name: The Watcher Arrives
description: Scholar Maren has been tracking the Watcher constellation's return to home position. Her models predict the night it completes the journey. She wants a witness at the observatory.
accept_text: 'Tonight.' She says it with the certainty of someone who has checked the calculations enough times that certainty is the appropriate response. 'The Watcher has been traveling back to home position for — depending on which observer's records you trust — between eighty and ninety years. The rate of movement accelerated thirty years ago. The rate accelerated again eight months ago, when the Grid reactivated.' She looks at the sky. 'The movement isn't random. The Watcher constellation's return to home position tracks with the Grid's healing curve. As the Grid recovers coherence, the movement rate increases.' She is quiet. 'The models converge on tonight as the night the Watcher reaches home position. Not approximately tonight. Tonight. This specific night.' She meets your eyes. 'I want someone at the observatory when it happens. I want a witness who will have spoken to Daxis about the Spire data, who knows what the Grid's healing process means, who can see the Watcher complete its journey with that context.' She pauses. 'I've been working this problem for three years. Tonight my part of the work finishes, and I need to know that someone who understands the whole picture is there when it does.' She looks at the observatory. 'Go up. Wait for it. You'll know when it happens. The instruments will respond before your eyes do, but your eyes will follow. The Watcher will be exactly where it started, exactly where it was when the Elder People built the first node.' She pauses. 'Three hundred years after everything went wrong. It arrives tonight.'
complete_text: The instruments in the observatory begin responding before the sky changes visibly. The large crystal at the center of the room, which you have seen pulse gently since you first came here, pulses once — and stays bright. Not a pulse. A sustained output. The pilot Kira, who has stayed at the observatory since her own discovery, looks at the crystal and says: 'There it is.' When you look at the sky the Watcher is home. The shape the constellation makes is different from the shape you've seen it make on other nights — it is in the position it was designed to occupy, the position from which the Elder People read it when the Grid was complete. You are not an astronomer and you may not have the vocabulary for what you're seeing. What you have is this: three hundred years ago something was broken badly enough that it changed the sky. Tonight the sky changed back. The crystal's sustained brightness is the construction wave at full coherence — the Grid is not healed, not finished, but the primary node and the Spire node are broadcasting at aligned frequency for the first time since the Cascade, and the network is responding. Kira says, quietly: 'The timing intervals I've been recording. The warmings. They stop here. They stop because the cycle is complete.' She looks at the crystal. 'The interval was counting down.' She is quiet for a long time. Then: 'What happens next?' And you don't have an answer, but the crystal holds its light steadily through the whole of the night, and you stay at the instruments and watch the Watcher hold its position against the turning sky, and by morning you have something you didn't have before: the specific shape of what is no longer broken.
